content.text: Instruction tuning (supervised fine-tuning on instructions) teaches models to
follow user directions

in conversational form—how to answer questions, refuse unsafe requests, and respond in chat format. Instruct checkpoints are the usual starting point for agent systems; raw base models rarely follow tool schemas reliably without this stage.
What it is
A dataset of (instruction, input, response) triples or multi-turn chats is used to minimize standard LM loss on
assistant tokens only
(prompt tokens masked in loss). Models learn role structure: system, user, assistant, tool.
Why it matters

Pre-trained models predict continuations of web text—not helpful Q&A. Instruction tuning aligns surface behavior with user intent, enabling zero-shot following of prompts like "summarize in bullet points" or "output only JSON." Agents depend on this for parsing natural-language goals into structured actions.
How it works
Collect diverse instructions: brainstorming, extraction, coding, safety refusals
Mix human-written and synthetic (distilled) data
Train with masked loss on assistant segments
Evaluate with benchmarks (MT-Bench, IFEval) and task-specific agent evals
Benefits
Large jump in usability from base → instruct with moderate data
Enables zero-shot and few-shot agent prompts without full custom FT
Foundation for later RLHF / DPO alignment
Trade-offs
Sycophancy—models may agree rather than push back
Format over substance if synthetic data is shallow
Does not guarantee factual accuracy or safe tool use without further alignment
Instruction tuning vs agent fine-tuning
Instruction tuning is broad; agent fine-tuning narrows to tool traces and domain workflows. Production stacks: pre-train → instruction tune → (optional) domain FT → alignment (RLHF/DPO).
Real-world example

An instruct model follows "Respond with valid JSON only" much more often than a base model, reducing validator failures in a customer-support agent—even before domain-specific fine-tuning on ticket archives.
Key takeaways
Instruction tuning teaches following directions in chat format
Loss applied on assistant tokens; prompts masked
Instruct checkpoints are the default backbone for agents
Further alignment needed for reliable tool safety and preferences
